children.name: Dicrurus
children.description: The typical drongos — a genus of medium-sized, entirely black or dark grey passerines with deeply forked tails, found across Africa, Asia, and Australasia. They are aggressive, fearless birds that will attack much larger species that approach their nests, including raptors and crows. They are accomplished aerial insectivores and noted vocal mimics, incorporating the calls of other birds into their own repertoire.

children.name: Dicrurus macrocercus
children.description: A glossy black drongo with a deeply forked tail, one of the most common and familiar birds of South and Southeast Asia, found in open country, farmland, and urban areas. It is an aggressive insectivore that hawks for prey from exposed perches and fearlessly mobs larger birds, including crows and raptors. Its wide distribution and adaptability have made it a familiar companion to livestock, catching insects disturbed by grazing animals.

children.name: Dicrurus leucophaeus
children.description: A medium-sized drongo with pale grey plumage, a distinctive red eye, and a deeply forked tail, found in forests and woodlands across South and Southeast Asia. Its plumage varies dramatically across its range, from near-white to slate-grey. It is an accomplished mimic that incorporates the calls of other birds into its own varied song.

children.name: Dicrurus bracteatus
children.description: A glossy black drongo with iridescent blue-green spangles on the breast, head, and wings, and a deeply forked, outward-curving tail, found from the Moluccas through New Guinea to eastern Australia. It is a bold, aggressive bird that harries larger species and is an expert aerial insect hunter. Its name has entered Australian slang to describe a foolish person.

children.name: Dicrurus paradiseus
children.description: A spectacular drongo with elongated outer tail feathers that end in broad, racket-like tips, found in forests from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Borneo. It is one of the finest avian mimics in Asia, perfectly imitating the calls of other birds, mammals, and even mechanical sounds. It is often a core member of mixed-species feeding flocks, acting as a sentinel for other birds.
